coredata.dc:title: Effect of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots on the fibrillation of hen egg-white lysozyme
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Hua-jin
Miao, Min
Liu, Zhe
Yang, Ran
Qu, Ling-bo
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the fibrillation of hen egg-white lysozyme (HEWL) in the absence and presence of different amount of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was studied by Thioflavin T (ThT) spectroscopy, Congo red (CR) binding assays, 8-anilino-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (ANS) fluorescence assay, circular dichroism (CD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The experimental results indicated that not only the fibrillation of HEWL at high temperature (65°C) and low pH (pH=2.0) could be inhibited effectively by N-GQDs, but the inhibition of HEWL by N-GQDs followed a dose-dependent manner. The results of this work suggested that the N-GQDs had a great potential for designing new therapeutic agents and were promising for future treatment of amyloid-related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hen egg-white lysozyme
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Amyloid fibril
Inhibition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of N-GQDs on HEWL amyloid fibril formation
The effect of N-GQDs on HEWL surface hydrophobicity
The effect of N-GQDs on HEWL secondary structure
Influence of N-GQDs on HEWL morphology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We grateful acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U1304823 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characteration of N-GQDs. (a) TEM image of N-GQDs. (b) XPS spectra of N-GQDs. (c) High-resolution C 1s peaks 1, 2, 3 correspond to CC/CC in aromatic rings, CN(O), and CO groups, respectively. (d) High-resolution N 1s peaks.
The absorption and emission spectra of N-GQDs.
The kinetics of HEWL fibril formation as monitored by the ThT fluorescence in the absence or the presence of different amount of N-GQDs.

The absorption spectra of CR in native HEWL and native HEWL after incubation for 8days at the absence and presence of different concentrations of N-GQDs. Conditions: peaks from up to down: HEWL fibril, HEWL sample with 50μL N-GQDs, HEWL sample with 100μL N-GQDs, HEWL sample with 200μL N-GQDs and native HEWL.
The effect of N-GQDs on the hydrophobicity of HEWL after incubation for 8days at the absence and presence of different amount of N-GQDs.
The CD spectra of native HEWL and native HEWL after incubation for 8days at the absence and presence of different amount of N-GQDs (a) and the crystal structure of HEWL (b).
TEM images of native HWEL (a) and native HEWL after incubation for 8days at the absence (b) and presence of N-GQDs (200μL) (c).
Secondary structural element in HEWL in the absence and presence of N-GQDs.

row.entry: The amount of N-GQDs added to HEWL

head.ce:title: Effect of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots on the fibrillation of hen egg-white lysozyme

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the fibrillation of hen egg-white lysozyme (HEWL) in the absence and presence of different amount of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was studied by Thioflavin T (ThT) spectroscopy, Congo red (CR) binding assays, 8-anilino-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (ANS) fluorescence assay, circular dichroism (CD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The experimental results indicated that not only the fibrillation of HEWL at high temperature (65°C) and low pH (pH=2.0) could be inhibited effectively by N-GQDs, but the inhibition of HEWL by N-GQDs followed a dose-dependent manner. The results of this work suggested that the N-GQDs had a great potential for designing new therapeutic agents and were promising for future treatment of amyloid-related diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to its association with various diseases, such as Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, Huntington’s disease, Type II diabetes, dialysis-related amyloidosis and various forms of systemic amyloidosis [1–6], protein amyloid aggregation has attracted considerable interest in the fields of materials science and biotechnology [7]. The conversion of a specific protein or protein fragment from soluble native state into insoluble amyloid fibrils results in the formation of protein deposits in a variety of organs and tissues with a single predominant protein component that is characteristic of each disease [8–11]. Based on the pathological features of amyloid-related disease, several therapeutic approaches, including direct inhibition of the self-assembly processes, attenuating the production of amyloidogenic forms, increasing the stability of the native structure of proteins and finally raising the clearance rate of aggregated protein have been suggested so far to deal with amyloidogenic diseases [12,13]. Among these approaches, developing anti-aggregating or anti-amyloidogenic agents was regarded as a potential strategy to battle amyloidoses. Therefore, in recent years, many reagents, including some small molecular drugs [14–22] and nanomaterials [23–26], were investigated to evaluate their influence on protein aggregation.

Hen egg-white lysozyme (HEWL), a well-characterized protein, is a globular protein with 129 amino acid residues that comprise two different domains cross-linked by means of four disulfide bonds [27,28]. Under in vitro conditions of low pH and elevated temperature, HEWL is able to transform into amyloid fibrils with typical fibrillar morphology and increased binding capacities for Thioflavin T (ThT) and Congo Red (CR) [29,30]. The human lysozyme has nearly a 60% homology with the HEWL [28], therefore, HEWL has been used as an alternative in vitro model for studying amyloidogenesis of a protein [31–35].

Nitrogen doping has been a powerful method to modulate the properties of carbon materials for various applications and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have gained remarkable interest because of their unique chemical, electronic and optical properties [36]. As a promising biomaterial, it is very significant to investigate the effect of N-GQDs on protein aggregation/fibrillazation. Thus, in order to further accumulate the effect of nanomaterials, especially N-GQDs on protein aggregation, in this study we used HEWL as a prototypic amyloidogenic protein to investigate the influence of as-prepared N-GQDs on HEWL aggregation. Furthermore, the interactions between N-GQDs and HEWL were investigated by ThT fluorescence spectroscopy, CR binding assay, 8-anilino-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (ANS) fluorescence assay, circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy (TEM).

HEWL (EC 3.2.1.17) (lyophilized powder) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Thioflavin T (ThT), 8-anilino-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (ANS) and Congo red (CR) were obtained from Aladdin Reagents Company (Shanghai, China). Natural graphite flakes with the average diameter of 325 meshes were purchased from Alfa Aesar (Lancs, UK). All the other chemicals and reagents used in the experiments were of analytical grade.

The N-GQDs were synthesized by carbonization of citric acid with ammonia through hydrothermal treatment according to the literature reported previously [36] with some modifications. 20mL of 1mgmL−1 citric acid solution and 4.0mL of 25% (w/v) ammonia solution were transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and were heated at 200°C for 3h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the pH of the solution for preparing N-GQDs was adjusted to 8.0 by adding NaOH solution under stirring. The obtained mixture was dialyzed in a dialysis tubing (3000Da, Spectrum Lab, Inc.) against deionization water for 4h to remove impurities and excess ammonia. After dialysis, the pure N-GQDs solution was obtained and stored in a refrigerator at 4°C for further characterization and use.

HEWL fibrils were prepared according to Naik method with some modification [37]. HEWL sample solutions (1.47mgmL−1) were prepared in hydrochloric acid buffer (pH 2.0) containing 136.7mmolL−1 NaCI and 2.68mmolL−1 KCI in the absence and presence of different amount of as-prepared N-GQDs (50μL, 100μL and 200μL). To induce the production of the amyloid structure, HEWL solutions were incubated for 8days at 65°C in a water bath without agitation. The formation of HEWL aggregates was monitored by ThT fluorescence assay, CR binding assay, ANS fluorescence assay, CD spectroscopy and TEM.

HEWL fibril formation was monitored by characteristic changes in ThT fluorescence intensity. At different time intervals, an aliquot (100μL) of incubated HEWL sample was mixed with 900μL of 10μmolL−1 ThT solutions, mixed thoroughly, and incubated for 1min. The fluorescence intensity was carried out on a RF-5301 Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at room temperature. Fluorescence spectra were then taken using excitation at 440nm. The excitation and emission slit widths were set at 3 and 5nm, respectively.

At different time intervals, 400μL of each HEWL sample was added to 3.6mL of 20μmolL−1 CR solution in 10mmolL−1 PBS (pH=7.4) and incubated at room temperature for 30min before measurement. The spectra of samples were acquired from 400 to 700nm using T6 UV–vis Spectrophotometer (Purkinje General, Beijing, China) at 25°C. All measurements were taken in triplicate.

100μL of each HEWL sample solution was mixed with 900μL of 10μmolL−1 ANS solution and then the mixture was incubated for 1h at room temperature. ANS fluorescence intensity was measured using a RF-5301 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) by exciting each sample at 380nm, and the emission was recorded between 420 and 600nm. The fluorescence intensity between 470 and 490nm was employed for the determination of the hydrophobicity of fibril in the different samples. Excitation and emission slits widths were set at 3 and 5nm, respectively.

CD spectra were recorded on chirascan TM spectrometer (Applied Photophysics Ltd.) with a 1.0mm quartz cell in the range of 190–260nm at 25°C. The acquisition parameters included step intervals of 0.3nm, scanning rate of 50nms−1, and average time of 2s. Three scans of each duplicate sample were recorded and averaged. Control buffer scans were run in duplicate, averaged and then subtracted from the sample spectra. The results were plotted as ellipticity versus wavelength. The secondary structure contents of the samples were estimated using CD Pro software.

For TEM analysis, 10μL of sample solution was placed on a 300-mesh carbon coated stabilized, formvar coated grids. After adsorption for 2min, the sample was washed with distilled water and air-dried. Each grid was then stained with 2% (w/v) uranyl acetate for 2min excess stain was removed, and the sample was again allowed to air-dry. Finally, each grid was viewed with a CEM 902A Zeiss microscope (Oberkochen, Germany).

In this study, a facile strategy was established to prepare N-GQDs by hydrothermal treatment on citric acid at 200°C for 3.0h. Here, citric acid and ammonia acted as the carbon source and functional group, respectively. The morphology and size of the N-GODs were characterized by using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the prepared N-GQDs were all highly dispersed and the size of N-GQDs were estimated to be about 10nm. The spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the N-GQDs exhibited three peaks at 285.48, 399.17, and 530.68eV (shown in Fig. 1b), which were attributed to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s could be well deconvoluted into four surface components (shown in Fig. 1c), corresponding to sp2 (CC/CC) at binding energy of 284.4eV, CN at 285.5eV, CO at 287.2eV, and CO at 288.3eV. The atomic percentage of C, N and O were found to be 54.3%, 21.15% and 24.55%, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of N-GQDs (shown in Fig. 1c) showed that there are three peaks at 284.04, 285.43 and 287.38eV, corresponding to CC/CC in aromatic rings, CN(O) and COOH groups, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of N-GQDs (shown in Fig. 1d) showed that there is a peak at 399.18eV, corresponding to CN [38]. From the above mentioned, it was concluded that the N-GQDs have been successfully synthesized.

To explore the optical properties of the N-GQDs, the UV–vis absorption and excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of N-GQDs were investigated and the results were shown in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-GQDs gave a shoulder peak at about 235nm and a characteristic absorption peak at 343nm, which were attributed to the π-π* transitions of the CC bonds and the n-π* transitions of the CO bonds, respectively. The results of photoluminescent spectra of N-GQDs indicated that the emission wavelength of N-GQDs was nearly excitation-independent. With increasing excitation wavelength, the emission peak position remains unchanged, but the intensity has changed obviously. The maximum emission intensity from N-GQDs was achieved at 440nm when they excited at 345nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of N-GQDs on HEWL amyloid fibril formation
ce:section.ce:para: The conversion of HEWL monomer to mature fibrils was evaluated and confirmed using common techniques of amyloid detection such as ThT assay which leads to enhance fluorescence after binding to the cross β-sheet-containing amyloids [12,39]. To examine the impact of N-GQDs on HEWL fibrillation, the HEWL was incubated statically with N-GQDs and without N-GQDs at high temperature and low pH for 8days. Aliquots from the HEWL sample solutions were taken at different time points, and the fluorescence of ThT was measured. As shown in Fig. 3 , the process of HEWL aggregation illustrates three stages of a typical protein fibrillation, namely, the lag phase, elongation phase and steady state. In the lag phase, the HEWL aggregated into small clusters. The ThT fluorescence intensity increased slowly for the first few hours when a fresh HEWL alone was incubated at 65°C. Previous studies also showed that the lag phase was a rate-limiting step [40,41]. After one day, a lot of clusters aggregated into amyloid fibril and the ThT fluorescence intensity started to rise rapidly, and then reached the fluorescence plateau until the fifth day. These results were in agreement with nucleation-dependent polymerization model of most amyloidogenic proteins [42,43]. However, when added N-GQDs into HEWL solution, not only the lag phase was prolonged to two days, but also ThT fluorescence intensity of the fibrillar HEWL was significantly quenched in the presence of N-GQDs dose-dependently, implying the dissolution or dispersion of the β-sheet structures by N-GQDs.

Amyloid formation and disaggregation was also evaluated by CR absorbance approach, which after binding to the cross-β structure, exhibits a red shift-associated absorbance enhancement [13,39,44]. As seen in Fig. 4 , CR alone in the native HEWL solution gave a maximal absorption peak at 490nm. However, once CR was added to the HEWL solution containing amyloid fibril, an increase in CR absorption accompanied with a red-shift of the spectral maximum from 490nm to 510nm was observed. Moreover, the HEWL sample displayed a second shoulder peak at around 540nm, which is indicative of a strong binding affinity between CR dye and HEWL, thus, signifying the presence of a substantial amount of amyloid fibrils [45]. However, relative to HEWL itself, the attenuations of spectral changes were observed to depend on the amount of N-GQDs added (50μL∼200μL). Thus, the ThT fluorescence and CR binding results suggested that decreased fibril formation was correlated to increased reductant amount.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of N-GQDs on HEWL surface hydrophobicity
ce:section.ce:para: ANS, a hydrophobic, aromatic and charged fluorescent probe, is widely used in studying the molten globule intermediate state (partially folded state) in protein folding experiments. It is also used to determine the binding sites of proteins and detecting exposed nonpolar surfaces in protein solutions. The preferential binding of ANS to hydrophobic clusters gives rise to an enhancement in fluorescence emission that accompanies a blue shift of the emission maxima and a dramatic increase in quantum yield [46–48]. As a matter of fact, ANS has been employed to indirectly probe for the information of surface hydrophobicity in a significant body of research associated with the formation of amyloid fibrils induced by acidic pH. Therefore, in exploring the effect of N-GQDs on the change in surface hydrophobicity of HEWL, we monitored the ANS fluorescence emission spectra upon excitation at 380nm. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity of native HEWL was remarkably low. This low intensity is due to the hidden hydrophobic sites within the native protein [49]. However, a marked increase in ANS fluorescence intensity was observed after fibrillization of HEWL for 8days, suggesting the exposure of hydrophobic patches on the surface of the protein. However, as illustrated in Fig. 5, simultaneous incubation of HEWL with different amount of N-GQDs significantly lowered ANS fluorescence dose-dependently, indicating the attenuation of exposed hydrophobic regions on the fibrils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of N-GQDs on HEWL secondary structure
ce:section.ce:para: Concerning the fact that aggregation of proteins into amyloid fibrils is accompanied by a conformational change from α-helical mode to β-sheet [39], the CD absorption spectroscopy would be an ideal approach to predict and document this transition. Fig. 6 a showed the far UV CD spectra of native HEWL, HEWL fibril (8days old) and HEWL solutions with different amount of N-GQDs. The native HEWL exhibited a strong negative band at 208nm, which indicated that the native HEWL consisted of significant amount of α-helical structure [50]. This result could be also confirmed in its crystal structure of HEWL (shown in Fig. 6b) [28]. However, when the sample was incubated at 65°C for 8days, the minimum was changed to 202nm, indicating that HEWL sample contained a large number of β-sheet conformation and predominantly disordered structure. After incubation with different amount of N-GQDs, the shape of spectra did not change significantly, but the negative delta epsilon decreased, indicating that the secondary structure of HEWL had change. The calculated contents of different secondary structure using CD Pro software were listed in Table 1 . As shown in Table 1, the addition of N-GQDs appeared to prevent HEWL from forming amyloid fibril associated β-sheet-rich conformation and the observed preventive effect seemed to be correlated with the amount of N-GQDs. These results strongly reconfirm the results obtained from the ThT fluorescence and CR absorbance assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of N-GQDs on HEWL morphology
ce:section.ce:para: In parallel with the spectroscopic analysis, the species formed from incubation with and without N-GQDs were morphologically analyzed with TEM. Fig. 7 a, b and c showed representative electron micrographs of native HEWL, HEWL fibril (8days old) and HEWL solution with addition of 200μL N-GQDs (incubation at 65°C for 8days), respectively. As seen in Fig. 7a, no aggregated/fibrillar species were found in fresh samples of HEWL. In the absence of N-GQDs, long, thick and hair-liked fibrils were observed after the HEWL solution was incubated for 8day at 65°C (shown in Fig. 7b). However, in the presence of 200μL N-GQDs, the amount of fibrils was obviously reduced. Moreover, the fibrils that were produced in the presence of N-GQDs appear thinner than those formed in the absence of N-GQDs (shown in Fig. 7c). These results are corroborated with ThT fluorescence, CR absorbance assays, ANS fluorescence assay, and CD measurements, which all tend to lead to the same conclusion: aggregation/fibrillazation can be inhibited effectively if the N-GQDs are present in HEWL solution before the aggregation begins. However, in order to gain a better understanding on how exactly these N-GQDs interact with biological molecules, more detailed investigations must be conducted which focus on how these protein-N-GQDs interactions can be controlled and the potential nanotoxicity of N-GQDs in a biological system.

In this paper, a facile strategy was successfully established to prepare N-GQDs by hydrothermal treatment on citric acid at 200°C for 3.0h, and then the effect of as-prepared N-GQDs on amyloid aggregation of HEWL was investigated by using ThT fluorescence spectroscopy, CR binding assay, ANS fluorescence assay, CD spectroscopy and TEM. The experimental results indicated that not only the fibrillation of HEWL at high temperature (65°C) and low pH (pH=2.0) could be inhibited effectively by N-GQDs, but the inhibition of HEWL by N-GQDs followed a dose-dependent manner. CD spectra showed that the addition of N-GQDs appeared to prevent HEWL from forming amyloid fibril associated β-sheet-rich conformation. All these experimental results in this paper would be helpful in understanding the mechanism of inhibitory effect of N-GQDs against the fibrillation of HEWL.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We grateful acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1304823).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A.
